name: Azure SDK for Python
what_it_does: Microsoft Azure's Python SDK, composed of 180+ individual service libraries covering Azure Blob Storage, Cosmos DB, Azure OpenAI, Azure Functions, Key Vault, Active Directory, and all other Azure services. Split into management (control plane) and client (data plane) libraries. Essential for agents operating in Azure environments.
use_cases: Reading and writing to Azure Blob Storage for agent file workflows
Accessing Azure OpenAI Service endpoints for LLM inference
Storing and retrieving agent state in Cosmos DB or Table Storage
Retrieving secrets from Azure Key Vault during agent initialization
Managing Azure resources programmatically (VMs, App Services, AKS)
Publishing events to Azure Service Bus or Event Grid for async coordination
Using Azure AI Services (Cognitive Services, Azure AI Search)
not_for: Teams without an Azure subscription or Microsoft account
Environments requiring a single small-footprint dependency
best_when: An agent runs in Azure or an enterprise Microsoft environment and needs to interact with Azure services including Azure OpenAI, Blob Storage, Cosmos DB, or Key Vault.
avoid_when: Your infrastructure is on AWS or GCP, or you're not in a Microsoft-heavy enterprise environment.

auth.notes: DefaultAzureCredential is the recommended pattern — chains through managed identity (for Azure-hosted agents), environment variables (AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET, AZURE_TENANT_ID), Azure CLI, and developer tools automatically. Managed Identity (system or user-assigned) is the gold standard for Azure-hosted agents — keyless auth with no secret management. Service Principal + client secret or certificate for external agents.

pricing.free_tier_limits: SDK is free and open-source; costs per Azure service used (Blob Storage first 5GB LRS free, Cosmos DB first 1,000 RU/s free tier available)
pricing.paid_tiers: Pay-per-use per service — Blob Storage from $0.018/GB, Cosmos DB from $0.008/RU-hour, Azure OpenAI usage-based
pricing.notes: SDK libraries are open-source and free. Azure services are pay-as-you-go with a $200 free credit for new accounts. Enterprise customers often have volume discounts through EA agreements. Azure OpenAI costs can be significant for high-throughput agent workloads.

performance.rate_limits: Per-service: Blob Storage 20,000 requests/s per account, Cosmos DB throughput-based (provisioned RU/s), Service Bus 10MB/s per namespace

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Raises azure.core.exceptions with typed exceptions (HttpResponseError, ResourceNotFoundError, ClientAuthenticationError). Error responses include error code, message, and correlation ID for support. Authentication errors are well-described but credential chain failures can be hard to debug — use logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG) to trace DefaultAzureCredential resolution.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Blob upload (PutBlob) is idempotent. Cosmos DB upsert operations are idempotent. Service Bus supports duplicate detection windows for message deduplication. ARM deployments support idempotent what-if mode. Many operations lack explicit idempotency keys.
known_agent_gotchas: DefaultAzureCredential tries many credential sources in order — silent fallbacks can cause agents to authenticate with wrong identity
Each Azure service is a separate pip package (azure-storage-blob, azure-cosmos, etc.) — version compatibility must be managed
Managed Identity requires the identity to have appropriate RBAC role assignments — often misconfigured
azure-identity package must be installed separately for DefaultAzureCredential — not bundled with service packages
Long-running operations return pollers — agents must call .result() or .wait() to get final state
Cosmos DB throughput limits cause RequestRateTooLarge (429) errors under load — partition key design is critical
ARM deployments are eventually consistent — resource availability after creation may lag by seconds
Connection strings vs AAD auth: connection strings are simpler but less secure; prefer managed identity in production

security_score_components.security_notes: Managed identities preferred for Azure VMs/containers. Service principals with RBAC for automation. Azure Key Vault for secrets. SOC2, ISO27001, FedRAMP, HIPAA. Active Directory integration. Zero-trust architecture support.
